answer:
We test for invalidity by trying to make all premises true and the conclusion false.  
If we set I true, J false, H true, and K true, then premise 1 (H ≡ (I · ~J)) is true, premise 2 (K ≡ ~H) is false, so this assignment fails.  
The correct counterexample is when I and H are true and J and K are false, making all premises true and the conclusion false.  

\boxed{C}
